en.extract:
The blue-throated bee-eater (Merops viridis) is a species of bird in the bee-eater family. It is found in much of southeast Asia, in subtropical or tropical mangrove forests. Their diet consists mostly of bees, wasps, and dragonflies. Blue-throated bee-eater is a medium-large species of bee-eater with brightly coloured plumage consisting of a red-brown nape, dark green wings, light green breast, and their signature blue throat. The juvenile plumage has a dark green head and wings and light green breast, only developing their full plumage in adulthood. They have a rich variety of songs and calls, including long calls which allow them to communicate long distances in the forest.
The conservation status of the blue-throated bee-eaters is of "least concern" due to their large distribution and stability of its population as of 2024. However, deforestation may be its biggest threat, destroying its habitat and decreasing bird diversity generally.


Taxonomy
The blue-throated bee-eater was formally described in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae under the current binomial name Merops viridis. The type locality is the Indonesian island of Java. The specific epithet viridis is Latin meaning "green". The species is monotypic, with no subspecies being recognised. It was however formerly considered to be conspecific with the rufous-crowned bee-eater (now M. americanus) of the Philippines as M. viridis americanus, until an analysis of Philippine birds recommended splitting it. An alternative common name is the chestnut-headed bee-eater.


Description

Adult blue-throated bee-eaters are around 21 cm long, excluding the elongated central tail feathers, which are an additional 9 cm, and a weight of around 34–41 grams. Adults have spectacular plumage with a red-brown crown and nape, dark green wings, blue tail, light green breast and belly, and the signature blue throat. Juveniles are mostly green all over their body, developing their full plumage later; they have a dark green head and wings and light green breast, and also lack the elongated central tail feathers of adults. Both adults and juveniles have black eye stripes. The eyes range between red and brown, or a combination.


Distribution and habitat
They have a wide distribution ranging from southeastern China to the Greater Sundas Islands. The most concentrated distribution is found in Singapore, Malaysia, southern Cambodia, and southern Thailand. Other locations with greater dispersal include Borneo and Java. It is most widespread at low altitudes, between 0–670 m, but is known to occur up to 1,600 m.
Their habitat includes a wide variety of flat plains, such as farmland, suburban gardens, riversides, dunes, and sandy clearings. In the winter, some blue-throated bee-eaters move to forest canopies and saltwater channels of mangrove forests. Unlike most bee-eaters which dig nest burrows in near-vertical sand cliffs, they often dig nest burrows nearly horizontally into flat ground. Colony sizes range from 50 to 200 pairs or living completely solitary in the open country.


Migration
Each spring, blue-throated bee-eaters (Merops viridis) along with blue-tailed bee-eaters (Merops philippinus) migrate from Sumatra, cross the Straits of Malacca, and end up on the west coast of Malaysia. The observed flight locomotion from Tanjung Tuan, west coast of Malaysia, was a combination of flapping and gliding flight. Occasionally, they would use the air currents from the sea-breeze to soar upwards. Once reaching land, they would rest on the lighthouse and tree branches for up to ten minutes before continuing eastward. This migration was observed from 2000 to 2001 with a total of 2,226 bee-eaters, including 1353 blue-tailed bee-eaters and 222 blue-throated bee-eaters, with the rest unidentified. The most observed migrating bee-eaters occurred on 21 March 2000 between 1 and 2PM. The high number observations of bee-eaters were most likely due to the strong thermals that formed over Sumatra, allowing them to soar over the sea-breeze with ease. High numbers of bee-eaters were also observed to migrate when there were high westerly winds blowing towards Malaysia. Blue-throated bee-eaters also migrate onto breeding grounds of western China during breeding season as shown in the range map above.

There is one record of vagrancy, a single in a blue-tailed bee-eater (Merops philippinus) colony near Kannur, Kerala, in southern India, from 26 May to 8 June 2013.


Behaviour and ecology


Vocal behaviour
Blue-throated bee-eaters make a combination of calls characterised as long calls, alarm calls, chirps, low chirps, purrs, sharp coos, trills, and feeding calls. Long calls have been observed to communicate long distances and can be recognised by their volume and intensity. A long call is performed either during flight or on a perch by stretching and pointing their bill upwards, known as a "long call" posture. Chirps are short and sharp with regular intervals, often used during digs.


Breeding

There has been several observations of migration between islands in southeast Asia or to mainland of Asia. One notable seasonal spring migration occurs from Sumatra, across the Strait of Malacca, and ending on the west coast of Malaysia. They also migrate from southeast Asia to breeding grounds in southern China during breeding season.
Blue-throated bee-eaters have a generation rate of around 6.2 years. They practice asynchronous brooding, with the parents beginning incubation with the first egg laid. This results in hatching of chicks at different times. The eggs hatch over a period of ten days with an average spread of 4.43 ±12.15 days. The sequence and timing of the hatching of chicks is correlated with size, with the first-born chick having the greatest mass. Parents lay two to seven eggs with a survival of zero to three chicks raised to fledging. The chicks normally die in order, starting from youngest and smallest. The observed death rate in chicks was largely caused by sibling attacks by using a sharp hook on the upper bill, later lost in development, and inflicting wounds on the naked head of other chicks. Those chicks who are older have time to grow more contour feathers, protecting them from damaging attacks. Siblicide is common among other birds to increase the larger and older chick's survival with greater access to food by the parents. Sibling attacks are more common among birds when food is scarce and monopolized. In the blue-throated bee-eater's case, insects are delivered one by one to the chicks, making food given to chicks with the greater advantage. Increasing brood size did not increase the survival of the chicks. Decreasing body mass is correlated with increasing wounds and scars found on the individual chicks, which increased the likelihood of death.


Food and feeding
They predominantly feed on flying insects such as bees and wasps, though a large percentage of their diet consists of dragonflies, with highest success rate of their catches in sunny conditions. Other insects caught include flies, beetles, and other bugs up to 42mm. Observations found no feeding activities during rain and right after showers. Their feeding patterns are well-matched with the seasonal weather patterns. The highest feeding rate is during breeding season, perfectly matching the sunniest period, right after the rainy season.


Relationship to humans
Humans act as a threat to blue-throated bee-eater habitats. The species has been listed as "least concern" in terms of conservational status, which is determined by a combination of range distribution, population stability, habitat loss, and potential threats; this was last assessed on 12 June 2024. Humans have impacted avian richness in the hill dipterocarp tropical rainforests in Malaysia. Logging and destruction of rainforests can impact not only individual bird species, but also the diversity of birds in the region. Diversity of species can bounce back within thirty years post-logging and was observed to have higher species richness in terms of bird diversity and numbers compared to recently logged forests.


References

The '''blue-throated bee-eater''' ('''''Merops viridis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the [[bee-eater]] family. It is found in much of [[southeast Asia]], in subtropical or tropical [[mangrove]] forests. Their diet consists mostly of [[bee]]s, [[wasp]]s, and [[Dragonfly|dragonflies]]. Blue-throated bee-eater is a medium-large species of bee-eater with brightly coloured plumage consisting of a red-brown [[nape]], dark green wings, light green breast, and their signature blue throat. The juvenile plumage has a dark green head and wings and light green breast, only developing their full plumage in adulthood. They have a rich variety of [[Bird vocalization|songs]] and calls, including long calls which allow them to communicate long distances in the forest.

The conservation status of the blue-throated bee-eaters is of "least concern" due to their large distribution and stability of its population as of 2024. However, deforestation may be its biggest threat, destroying its habitat and decreasing bird diversity generally.

==Taxonomy==
The blue-throated bee-eater was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' under the current [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Merops viridis''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | volume=1 | edition=10th | page=117 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii | location=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727022 }}</ref><ref name=peters>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1945 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=5 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=237 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480248 }}</ref> The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] is the Indonesian island of [[Java]].<ref name=peters/> The specific epithet ''viridis'' is [[Latin]] meaning "green".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=403 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n403/mode/1up }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]], with no [[subspecies]] being recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web | title=Todies, motmots, bee-eaters – IOC World Bird List | website=IOC World Bird List – Version 14.2 | date=2025-02-20 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/todies/ | access-date=2025-12-15}}</ref> It was however formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[rufous-crowned bee-eater]] (now ''M. americanus'') of the Philippines as ''M. viridis americanus'', until an analysis of Philippine birds recommended splitting it.<ref name="Collar">{{cite journal | last=Collar | first=Nigel J | title=Species limits in some Philippine birds including the Greater Flameback Chrysocolaptes lucidus | journal=Forktail | volume=27 | date=2011 | pages=29–38 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/283595 | access-date=2025-12-14}}</ref><ref name=ioc/> An alternative common name is the chestnut-headed bee-eater.<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last=Fry | first=C.H. | year=2001 | chapter=Family Meropidae (Bee-eaters) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=6: Mousebirds to Hornbills | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-87334-30-6 | pages=286–341 [338–339] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0006unse/page/338/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref>

==Description ==
[[File:Blue-throated Bee-eater Merops viridis, juvenile, Kinabatangan, Sabah, Malaysia 1.jpg|left|thumb|Juvenile, in [[Sabah]], Malaysia]]
Adult blue-throated bee-eaters are around 21 cm long, excluding the elongated central tail feathers, which are an additional 9 cm, and a weight of around 34–41 grams.<ref name=hbw/> Adults have spectacular plumage with a red-brown crown and nape, dark green wings, blue tail, light green breast and belly, and the signature blue throat. Juveniles are mostly green all over their body, developing their full plumage later; they have a dark green head and wings and light green breast, and also lack the elongated central tail feathers of adults. Both adults and juveniles have black eye stripes. The eyes range between red and brown, or a combination.<ref name="Stader">{{Cite journal|last=Stader|first=Lulu D.|date=1994|title=Breeding behaviour of a tropical bird: a study of the blue-throated Bee-eater (''Merops viridis'') using relational database and DNA fingerprinting.|url=http://dspace.stir.ac.uk/handle/1893/11893|journal=ETheses from Faculty of Natural Sciences Legacy Departments|language=en|via=STORRE}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
They have a wide distribution ranging from southeastern China to the [[Greater Sunda Islands|Greater Sundas Islands]].<ref name=hbw/> The most concentrated distribution is found in [[Singapore]], [[Malaysia]], southern [[Cambodia]], and southern [[Thailand]].<ref name=hbw/> Other locations with greater dispersal include [[Borneo]] and [[Java]].<ref name=hbw/> It is most widespread at low altitudes, between 0–670 m,<ref name="iucn"/> but is known to occur up to 1,600 m.<ref name="Hoyo">{{cite book | last=Hoyo | first=Josep del | title=All the Birds of the World | publisher=Lynx edicions | publication-place=Barcelona | date=2020 | isbn=978-84-16728-37-4 | page=299}}</ref>

Their habitat includes a wide variety of flat plains, such as farmland, suburban gardens, riversides, dunes, and sandy clearings.<ref name=hbw/> In the winter, some blue-throated bee-eaters move to forest canopies and saltwater channels of mangrove forests.<ref name=hbw/> Unlike most bee-eaters which dig nest burrows in near-vertical sand cliffs, they often dig nest burrows nearly horizontally into flat ground.<ref name="Stader" /> Colony sizes range from 50 to 200 pairs or living completely solitary in the open country.<ref name="Stader" />

=== Migration ===
Each spring, blue-throated bee-eaters (''Merops viridis'') along with blue-tailed bee-eaters (''Merops philippinus'') migrate from [[Sumatra]], cross the [[Strait of Malacca|Straits of Malacca]], and end up on the west coast of Malaysia.<ref name="DeCandido">{{Cite journal|last1=DeCandido|first1=Robert|last2=Allen|first2=Deborah|last3=Yosef|first3=Reuven|date=2004|title=Merops Migration at Tanjung Tuan, Malaysia: An Important Spring Bee-eater Migration Watchsite in South-east Asia|journal=Journal of the Yamashina Institute for Ornithology|volume=36|issue=1|pages=15–21|doi=10.3312/jyio.36.15|issn=1348-5032|doi-access=free}}</ref> The observed flight locomotion from Tanjung Tuan, west coast of Malaysia, was a combination of flapping and gliding flight.<ref name="DeCandido" /> Occasionally, they would use the air currents from the sea-breeze to soar upwards.<ref name="DeCandido" /> Once reaching land, they would rest on the lighthouse and tree branches for up to ten minutes before continuing eastward.<ref name="DeCandido" /> This migration was observed from 2000 to 2001 with a total of 2,226 bee-eaters, including 1353 blue-tailed bee-eaters and 222 blue-throated bee-eaters, with the rest unidentified.<ref name="DeCandido" /> The most observed migrating bee-eaters occurred on 21 March 2000 between 1 and 2PM.<ref name="DeCandido" /> The high number observations of bee-eaters were most likely due to the strong thermals that formed over Sumatra, allowing them to soar over the sea-breeze with ease.<ref name="DeCandido" /> High numbers of bee-eaters were also observed to migrate when there were high westerly winds blowing towards Malaysia.<ref name="DeCandido" /> Blue-throated bee-eaters also migrate onto breeding grounds of western China during breeding season as shown in the range map above.<ref name=hbw/>

[[File:Blue Throated Bee eater Kerala.jpg|thumb|The vagrant blue-throated Bee-eater in Kerala, India]]
There is one record of [[vagrancy (biology)|vagrancy]], a single in a [[blue-tailed bee-eater]] (''Merops philippinus'') colony near Kannur, Kerala, in southern India, from 26 May to 8 June 2013.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=Indian Birds |last1=Manekkara |first1=Sasidharan | title=Blue-throated Bee-eater ''Merops viridis'' in Kerala: An escapee, or a wild vagrant? |volume=12 |pages=76–78 | url=https://indianbirds.in/pdfs/IB_12_2_3_Manekkara_BluethroatedBeeEater.pdf | access-date=2025-12-14}}</ref>

== Behaviour and ecology ==
=== Vocal behaviour ===
Blue-throated bee-eaters make a combination of [[Bird vocalization|calls]] characterised as long calls, alarm calls, chirps, low chirps, purrs, sharp coos, trills, and feeding calls.<ref name="Stader" /> Long calls have been observed to communicate long distances and can be recognised by their volume and intensity.<ref name="Stader" /> A long call is performed either during flight or on a perch by stretching and pointing their bill upwards, known as a "long call" posture.<ref name="Stader" /> Chirps are short and sharp with regular intervals, often used during digs.<ref name="Stader" />

=== Breeding ===
[[File:Blue-throated Bee-eater Courtship offering.jpg|thumb|Courtship offering of an insect from male to female]]
There has been several observations of [[Bird migration|migration]] between islands in southeast Asia or to mainland of Asia. One notable seasonal spring migration occurs from Sumatra, across the Strait of Malacca, and ending on the west coast of Malaysia. They also migrate from southeast Asia to breeding grounds in southern China during breeding season.

Blue-throated bee-eaters have a generation rate of around 6.2 years.<ref name="iucn"/> They practice asynchronous brooding, with the parents beginning incubation with the first egg laid.<ref name="Bryant">{{Cite journal|last1=Bryant|first1=David M.|last2=Tatner|first2=Paul|date=1990-04-01|title=Hatching asynchrony, sibling competition and siblicide in nestling birds: Studies of swiftlets and bee-eaters|journal=Animal Behaviour|volume=39|issue=4|pages=657–671|doi=10.1016/S0003-3472(05)80377-X|s2cid=53195864|issn=0003-3472}}</ref> This results in hatching of chicks at different times. The eggs hatch over a period of ten days with an average spread of 4.43 ±12.15 days.<ref name="Bryant" /> The sequence and timing of the hatching of chicks is correlated with size, with the first-born chick having the greatest mass.<ref name="Bryant" /> Parents lay two to seven eggs with a survival of zero to three chicks raised to fledging.<ref name="Bryant" /> The chicks normally die in order, starting from youngest and smallest.<ref name="Bryant" /> The observed death rate in chicks was largely caused by sibling attacks by using a sharp hook on the upper bill, later lost in development, and inflicting wounds on the naked head of other chicks.<ref name="Bryant" /> Those chicks who are older have time to grow more contour feathers, protecting them from damaging attacks.<ref name="Bryant" /> Siblicide is common among other birds to increase the larger and older chick's survival with greater access to food by the parents.<ref name="Bryant" /> Sibling attacks are more common among birds when food is scarce and monopolized.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Mock|first=Douglas W.|date=1985|title=Siblicidal Brood Reduction: The Prey-Size Hypothesis|journal=The American Naturalist|volume=125|issue=3|pages=327–343|doi=10.1086/284346|bibcode=1985ANat..125..327M |s2cid=84218162|issn=0003-0147}}</ref> In the blue-throated bee-eater's case, insects are delivered one by one to the chicks, making food given to chicks with the greater advantage.<ref name="Bryant" /> Increasing brood size did not increase the survival of the chicks.<ref name="Bryant" /> Decreasing body mass is correlated with increasing wounds and scars found on the individual chicks, which increased the likelihood of death.<ref name="Bryant" />

=== Food and feeding ===
They predominantly feed on flying insects such as bees and wasps,<ref name="hbw"/> though a large percentage of their diet consists of [[Dragonfly|dragonflies]], with highest success rate of their catches in sunny conditions.<ref name="Stader" /> Other insects caught include flies, beetles, and other bugs up to 42mm.<ref name=hbw/> Observations found no feeding activities during rain and right after showers.<ref name="Stader" /> Their feeding patterns are well-matched with the seasonal weather patterns.<ref name="Stader" /> The highest feeding rate is during breeding season, perfectly matching the sunniest period, right after the rainy season.<ref name="Stader" />

== Relationship to humans ==
Humans act as a threat to blue-throated bee-eater habitats. The species has been listed as "least concern" in terms of conservational status, which is determined by a combination of range distribution, population stability, habitat loss, and potential threats; this was last assessed on 12 June 2024.<ref name="iucn"/> Humans have impacted avian richness in the hill dipterocarp tropical rainforests in Malaysia.<ref name="Rajpar">{{Cite journal|last1=Rajpar|first1=M. N.|last2=Zakaria|first2=M.|date=2014-09-01|journal=American Journal of Applied Sciences|volume=11|issue=9|pages=1519–1529|doi=10.3844/ajassp.2014.1519.1529|issn=1546-9239|title=Assessing the Effects of Logging Activities on Avian Richness and Diversity in Different Aged Post-Harvested Hill Dipterocarp Tropical Rainforest of Malaysia|doi-access=free}}</ref> Logging and destruction of rainforests can impact not only individual bird species, but also the diversity of birds in the region.<ref name="Rajpar" /> Diversity of species can bounce back within thirty years post-logging and was observed to have higher species richness in terms of bird diversity and numbers compared to recently logged forests.<ref name="Rajpar" />

zh.extract:
蓝喉蜂虎（学名：Merops viridis）为蜂虎科蜂虎属的鸟类。该物种的模式产地在爪哇。其种加词“viridis”意为“绿色的”。


亚种
蓝喉蜂虎指名亚种（学名：Merops viridis viridis）。在中国大陆，分布于云南、广西、广东、海南、湖南、江西、福建、河南等地。该物种的模式产地在爪哇。
